article_title: The one Slack message that proved our elite engineering team was flying blind
core_thesis: Multi-cloud environments are rarely the result of deliberate strategy; they accumulate organically through acquisitions, pilot projects, and individual team decisions. This leads to critical blind spots in infrastructure visibility that traditional IaC tools and cloud consoles cannot address. The article argues that a unified, queryable infrastructure inventory—built with tools like CloudQuery—is the foundational prerequisite for effective governance, security, cost control, and incident response in multi-cloud setups.
detailed_summary: The article recounts a real-world scenario where a simple Slack question—'What are we actually running across both cloud environments?'—exposed a massive visibility gap in an engineering team managing infrastructure across multiple cloud providers. Despite being 'serious engineers,' the team lacked a cohesive map of their stack. The author explains that multi-cloud is typically an accumulation of decisions (e.g., core product on AWS, SaaS vendor on GCP, acquisition on Azure) rather than a planned strategy, citing Flexera's 2024 report that 89% of enterprises run workloads across multiple public clouds. The core problem is that most IaC platforms only track resources deployed through their pipelines, leaving pre-existing, acquired, or manually provisioned resources invisible. The author attempted to build a spreadsheet but gave up due to low confidence and the sheer complexity of cross-referencing consoles, cost reports, and team knowledge. The solution presented is CloudQuery, an open-source tool that syncs real cloud state from AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, Cloudflare, and dozens of other providers into SQL tables. With a simple SQL query, the team could get a cross-provider resource count in minutes instead of hours. However, the article notes that even with unified inventory, you still need to know what to look for; at scale, manual queries become insufficient, and the system should proactively surface anomalies. The author concludes that inventory and visibility must be treated as foundational infrastructure, built before mergers, incidents, or audits force the issue.

entities.name: Joe Karlsson
entities.role: Author and developer advocate at env zero + CloudQuery; recounts personal experience with multi-cloud blind spots.

entities.name: CloudQuery
entities.role: Open-source tool that syncs cloud infrastructure state into SQL tables for unified querying across providers.

entities.name: env zero
entities.role: Cloud governance platform that merged with CloudQuery; sponsored the article.

entities.name: Flexera
entities.role: Published the 2024 State of the Cloud Report cited for multi-cloud adoption statistics.

key_data_points: A Slack question about what was actually running across two cloud environments took an hour of thread replies, three teams, and resulted in an answer with gaps rather than a definitive inventory. (🟢 Relevant to your DevOps/homelab needs: highlights the real-world pain of multi-cloud visibility that you may encounter in your own infrastructure.)

Flexera's 2024 State of the Cloud Report states that 89% of enterprises run workloads across multiple public clouds, up from 87% the previous year. (🟢 Useful context for understanding the scale of the problem; as a student, this statistic underscores the importance of multi-cloud skills.)

Most cross-provider IaC platforms only cover resources deployed through their pipelines; anything predating the pipeline, acquired, or provisioned outside it remains a blind spot. (🟢 Critical for Red Teaming: blind spots are attack surfaces—unmanaged resources may lack security patches or monitoring.)

The author spent a day trying to build a spreadsheet from consoles and cost reports but abandoned it due to low confidence in accuracy. Cost reports show billing line items, not actual running resources. (🟢 Demonstrates the inadequacy of manual methods; as a programmer, you can appreciate the need for automated, queryable solutions.)

CloudQuery syncs real cloud state into SQL tables (e.g., aws_ec2_instances, gcp_compute_instances) and allows cross-provider queries like counting instances by provider in minutes. (🟢 Directly actionable for your homelab/DevOps: you can use CloudQuery to inventory your own multi-cloud or hybrid setup.)

Even with unified inventory, at scale you need the system to surface anomalies proactively rather than relying on manually written queries. (🟢 Insight for building automated monitoring and alerting in your homelab.)

The author recommends agreeing on a single tagging schema (owner, environment, cost-center) before a merger closes and enforcing it from day one, as retroactive tagging is nearly impossible. (🟢 Practical advice for any multi-cloud or team environment; applicable to your own projects.)
methodology_steps: Identify the need for a unified infrastructure inventory across all cloud providers and accounts.
Choose an open-source tool like CloudQuery that supports syncing state from AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, Cloudflare, and other providers.
Configure CloudQuery source plugins for each provider and run a sync to pull real cloud state into a PostgreSQL database.
Write SQL queries to aggregate resources across providers, e.g., SELECT 'aws' AS provider, 'ec2_instance' AS resource_type, COUNT(*) FROM aws_ec2_instances UNION ALL ...
Use pre-built multi-cloud inventory reports if available, or extend queries to filter by tags, join cost data, and cross-reference IaC declarations.
Schedule recurring checks for untagged resources, publicly accessible assets, or orphaned compute to maintain visibility.
Implement proactive anomaly detection so the system surfaces issues without requiring manual query writing.
conclusions: Multi-cloud is typically an accumulated condition, not a deliberate strategy; organizations must treat inventory and visibility as foundational infrastructure to govern what they cannot see. (Supports the need for proactive tooling in your homelab/DevOps.)

Unified inventory tools like CloudQuery can reduce a multi-team, multi-provider audit from hours to minutes, enabling faster incident response, cost attribution, and compliance prep. (Directly applicable to your Red Teaming: rapid asset discovery is crucial for reconnaissance.)
Tagging consistency from day one is critical; retroactive tagging across merged organizations is impractical. (Practical advice for any infrastructure project you undertake.)
Even with a unified view, manual queries are insufficient at scale; systems should proactively surface anomalies to prevent blind spots. (Encourages building automated monitoring in your homelab.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in DevOps, homelabbing, and Red Teaming. As a programmer and aspiring security professional, you can directly apply the concept of unified infrastructure inventory to your own homelab—using CloudQuery to track resources across multiple clouds or on-premise environments. For Red Teaming, the article highlights that unmanaged or invisible resources are prime attack surfaces; knowing what exists is the first step in exploitation or defense. The SQL-based approach also appeals to your programming background, allowing you to write custom queries for asset discovery and anomaly detection. Finally, the practical advice on tagging and proactive monitoring can help you build more resilient and observable systems from the start.
extra_advice: Consider setting up CloudQuery in your homelab to inventory all your cloud and local resources. Then, write a few SQL queries to find 'orphaned' resources (e.g., old VMs, unattached disks) and practice exploiting them in a controlled environment—this directly trains your Red Teaming reconnaissance skills. Additionally, automate the inventory sync and anomaly detection using a cron job or a CI/CD pipeline to simulate real-world continuous monitoring.
